<answer>```cpp
// add+modify_eas_records.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <map>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <stdexcept>

#include "eas_common.hpp"
using namespace sqlgen;
using namespace sqlgen::literals;

// Helper function to get primary key information
std::pair<std::string, std::string> get_primary_key_info(const std::string& table_name) {
    static const std::map<std::string, std::pair<std::string, std::string>> pk_map = {
        {"Student", {"SNo", "VARCHAR"}},
        {"Course", {"CID", "INTEGER"}},
        {"Score", {"ScID", "INTEGER"}},
        {"Major", {"MID", "INTEGER"}}
    };
    
    auto it = pk_map.find(table_name);
    if (it == pk_map.end()) {
        throw std::invalid_argument("Unknown table: " + table_name);
    }
    return it->second;
}

template<typename T>
void add_eas_records(const std::string& table_name,
                     const std::vector<T>& records) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
    
    // Get column names from the struct using reflection
    auto field_names = rfl::named_tuple_t<T>::names();
    std::vector<std::string> columns;
    for (const auto& name : field_names) {
        columns.push_back(name);
    }
    
    // Build the INSERT statement
    auto insert_stmt = sqlgen::dynamic::Insert{table_name, columns};
    
    // Prepare the values
    std::vector<std::vector<std::optional<std::string>>> values;
    for (const auto& record : records) {
        auto record_tuple = rfl::to_named_tuple(record);
        std::vector<std::optional<std::string>> row;
        for (const auto& [field_name, value] : record_tuple) {
            if (value.has_value()) {
                if constexpr (std::is_same_v<decltype(value), const char*>) {
                    row.push_back(std::string(value.value()));
                } else if constexpr (std::is_same_v<decltype(value), std::string>) {
                    row.push_back(value);
                } else if constexpr (std::is_arithmetic_v<decltype(value)>) {
                    row.push_back(std::to_string(value.value()));
                } else {
                    // Handle other types
                    row.push_back(std::to_string(value.value()));
                }
            } else {
                row.push_back(std::nullopt);
            }
        }
        values.push_back(std::move(row));
    }
    
    // Execute the insert
    auto result = sqlgen::exec(conn, insert_stmt, values.begin(), values.end());
}

void modify_eas_records_by_id(const std::string& table_name,
                              const std::string& record_id,
                              const std::string& col_name,
                              const std::string& new_value) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
    
    // Get primary key information
    auto [pk_column, pk_type] = get_primary_key_info(table_name);
    
    // Convert record_id to appropriate type
    std::optional<std::string> pk_value;
    if (pk_type == "INTEGER") {
        try {
            int id_int = std::stoi(record_id);
            pk_value = std::to_string(id_int);
        } catch (const std::exception&) {
            throw std::invalid_argument("Invalid integer record_id: " + record_id);
        }
    } else if (pk_type == "VARCHAR") {
        pk_value = record_id;
    } else {
        throw std::invalid_argument("Unsupported primary key type: " + pk_type);
    }
    
    // Construct UPDATE statement
    auto update_stmt = sqlgen::dynamic::Update{
        table_name,
        {sqlgen::dynamic::Set{col_name, new_value}},
        sqlgen::dynamic::Where{pk_column + " = ?", {pk_value}}
    };
    
    // Execute the update
    auto result = sqlgen::exec(conn, update_stmt);
}

void delete_eas_records_by_id(const std::string& table_name,
                              const std::string& record_id) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
    
    // Get primary key information
    auto [pk_column, pk_type] = get_primary_key_info(table_name);
    
    // Convert record_id to appropriate type
    std::optional<std::string> pk_value;
    if (pk_type == "INTEGER") {
        try {
            int id_int = std::stoi(record_id);
            pk_value = std::to_string(id_int);
        } catch (const std::exception&) {
            throw std::invalid_argument("Invalid integer record_id: " + record_id);
        }
    } else if (pk_type == "VARCHAR") {
        pk_value = record_id;
    } else {
        throw std::invalid_argument("Unsupported primary key type: " + pk_type);
    }
    
    // Construct DELETE statement
    auto delete_stmt = sqlgen::dynamic::Delete{
        table_name,
        sqlgen::dynamic::Where{pk_column + " = ?", {pk_value}}
    };
    
    // Execute the delete
    auto result = sqlgen::exec(conn, delete_stmt);
}
```

This solution provides:
1. **add_eas_records**: Uses dynamic INSERT to add records to the specified table
2. **modify_eas_records_by_id**: Uses dynamic UPDATE to modify a specific record
3. **delete_eas_records_by_id**: Uses dynamic DELETE to remove a specific record
4. **Proper type handling** for different primary key types
5. **Error handling** for invalid inputs
6. **SQL injection prevention** through parameterized queries

The solution leverages the sqlgen library's dynamic query building capabilities to safely construct and execute database operations while maintaining type safety through template usage and proper input validation.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace batch_insert_products {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  float price;
  std::string category;
  int stock;
};

bool batch_insert_products(const std::vector<Product>& products) {
    return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
        .and_then([](auto conn) { return conn.drop<Product>(true); })  // Drop if exists
        .and_then([](auto conn) { return conn.create_table<Product>(); })  // Create table
        .and_then([](auto conn) { return conn.begin_transaction(); })  // Begin transaction
        .and_then([&products](auto conn) { return conn.insert(products); })  // Batch insert
        .and_then([](auto conn) { return conn.commit(); })  // Commit
        .transform([](auto conn) { return true; })  // Success case
        .or_else([](auto err) { return false; });  // Error case
}

}  // namespace batch_insert_products
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Database Connection**: Connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`
2. **Drop Table**: Drops the `Product` table if it exists using `drop<Product>(true)`
3. **Create Table**: Creates the `Product` table structure using `create_table<Product>()`
4. **Transaction Management**: Uses `begin_transaction()` to start a transaction and `commit()` to finalize it
5. **Batch Insertion**: Efficiently inserts all products using `insert(products)` within the transaction
6. **Error Handling**: Returns `false` if any operation fails, ensuring data consistency through transaction rollback
7. **Return Value**: Returns `true` only if all operations complete successfully

The solution leverages `sqlgen`'s functional programming style with `and_then` for chaining operations and `or_else` for error handling, providing a clean and efficient implementation for batch database operations.</answer>

#include <filesystem>

  }
}

            )

But wait, the sqlgen library's API might differ. The code example in the problem uses sqlgen::sqlite::connect(), which returns a Result<Connection>. Then, using and_then to chain operations. So the code should handle the connection properly.

Also, the data types in SQLite: VARCHAR is not a standard SQLite type, but SQLite uses TEXT. However, the problem specifies VARCHAR(10), so perhaps the code should use VARCHAR, but in SQLite, that's equivalent to TEXT. So the CREATE TABLE statements should use VARCHAR for the struct's string fields.

But in SQLite, the type affinity is determined by the declared type. So using VARCHAR(10) would give TEXT affinity. So that's acceptable.

Now, putting all together into the C++ code.

The structs are defined as above, and the function design_eas_database creates the tables.

So the complete code would be:

#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <optional>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace test_design_eas_database {

  struct Student {
    std::string SNo;
    std::string SName;
    std::optional<int> Sex;
    std::optional<std::string> Birthday;
    std::optional<int> Major;
    std::optional<float> Scorses;
    std::optional<std::string> Remark;
  };

  struct Course {
    std::optional<int> CID;
    std::string CName;
    std::optional<int> Term;
    std::optional<int> CTime;
    float Credit;
  };

  struct Score {
    std::string SNo;
    int CID;
    std::optional<float> Score;
  };

  void design_eas_database() {
    // Remove existing database file
    if (std::filesystem::exists("test_db.sqlite")) {
        std::filesystem::remove("test_db.sqlite");
    }
    
    // Connect to the database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create tables
    conn.and_then([&](auto db) {
        // Create Student table
        db.execute(R"(
            CREATE TABLE Student (
                SNo VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
                SName VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
                Sex INTEGER NULL DEFAULT 1,
                Birthday DATETIME NULL,
                Major INTEGER NULL,
                Scorses FLOAT NULL DEFAULT 0,
                Remark TEXT NULL
            )
        )");
        
        // Create Course table
        db.execute(R"(
            CREATE TABLE Course (
                CID INTEGER NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
                CName VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
                Term INTEGER NULL DEFAULT 1,
                CTime INTEGER NULL,
                Credit FLOAT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
            )
        )");
        
        // Create Score table
        db.execute(R"(
            CREATE TABLE Score (
                SNo VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,
                CID INTEGER NOT NULL,
                Score FLOAT NULL,
                PRIMARY KEY (SNo, CID),
                FOREIGN KEY (SNo) REFERENCES Student(SNo),
                FOREIGN KEY (CID) REFERENCES Course(CID)
            )
        )");
        
        return db;
    });
  }

}
```

Wait, but in the Score table, the SNo and CID are part of the primary key. The struct Score has SNo and CID as non-optional, which aligns with the NOT NULL constraints in the SQL.

Also, the foreign key constraints are specified in the CREATE TABLE statement for Score.

I think this should cover all the requirements. The code removes the existing database file, connects to SQLite, and creates the three tables with the specified schemas.

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <chrono>
#include <iomanip>
#include <sstream>

namespace query_active_users_posts {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
};

struct Post {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> post_id;
  int user_id;
  sqlgen::DateTime created_at;
};

struct ActiveUser {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "ActiveUserView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
};

std::vector<ActiveUser> query_active_users_posts() {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create tables
    conn.and_then(create_table<User>);
    conn.and_then(create_table<Post>);
    
    // Execute query and return results
    return conn.and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<ActiveUser>> | 
                        "SELECT DISTINCT u.user_id, u.username "
                        "FROM User u "
                        "INNER JOIN Post p ON u.user_id = p.user_id "
                        "WHERE DATE(p.created_at) >= DATE('2025-12-01') AND DATE(p.created_at) <= DATE('2025-12-31')") 
             .value();
}

}  // namespace query_active_users_posts
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database "test_db.sqlite"
2. Creates the User and Post tables using `create_table<>`
3. Executes a SQL query that:
   - Joins User and Post tables
   - Filters for posts created in December 2025 using `DATE()` functions
   - Returns distinct users with their IDs and usernames
4. Returns the results as a vector of `ActiveUser` objects

The solution handles the date range correctly by using SQLite's `DATE()` function to compare only the date portion of the `created_at` datetime field.</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>Okay, I need to write a function called query_course_enrollment_stats using the sqlgen library. Let me start by understanding the requirements.

First, the function should connect to an SQLite database named test_db.sqlite. Then, create the Course and Enrollment tables. After that, it needs to query the database to get the number of students enrolled per course and the average grade per course, ignoring NULL grades. The results should be returned as a vector of CourseStats.

Looking at the table structures, the Course table has course_id as primary key and title. The Enrollment table has enrollment_id as primary key, a foreign key to Course's course_id, and an optional grade. 

The reference SQL uses a LEFT JOIN between Course and Enrollment, groups by course_id and title, and counts enrollments and averages grades. Since grades can be NULL, AVG will ignore them automatically.

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_course_enrollment_stats {

struct Course {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> course_id;
  std::string title;
};

struct Enrollment {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> enrollment_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Course, "course_id"> course_id;
  std::optional<float> grade;
};

struct CourseStats {
  std::string title;
  int student_count;
  std::optional<double> avg_grade;
};

std::vector<CourseStats> query_course_enrollment_stats() {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Create tables if they don't exist
  sqlgen::create_table<Course>(conn);
  sqlgen::create_table<Enrollment>(conn);
  
  // Execute the query to get enrollment statistics
  const std::string sql = R"(
    SELECT c.title, COUNT(e.enrollment_id) as student_count, AVG(e.grade) as avg_grade
    FROM Course c
    LEFT JOIN Enrollment e ON c.course_id = e.course_id
    GROUP BY c.course_id, c.title
  )";
  
  auto result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::sqlite::execute_query(sql))
                   .and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<CourseStats>>);
  
  return result.value();
}

}  // namespace query_course_enrollment_stats
```

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_customer_purchase_history {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
  std::string email;
};

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
};

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Customer, "customer_id"> customer_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
  double total_amount;
};

struct OrderItem {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Order, "order_id"> order_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Product, "product_id"> product_id;
  int quantity;
};

struct PurchaseHistory {
  std::string product_name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
  int quantity;
};

std::vector<PurchaseHistory> query_customer_purchase_history(int customer_id) {
    // Connect to SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create tables if they don't exist
    conn.and_then([&](auto& c) { c.execute(sqlgen::to_sql<Customer>()); });
    conn.and_then([&](auto& c) { c.execute(sqlgen::to_sql<Product>()); });
    conn.and_then([&](auto& c) { c.execute(sqlgen::to_sql<Order>()); });
    conn.and_then([&](auto& c) { c.execute(sqlgen::to_sql<OrderItem>()); });
    
    // Execute the query to retrieve purchase history
    auto result = conn.and_then([&](auto& stmt) {
        stmt.execute(
            R"(SELECT p.name, o.order_date, oi.quantity FROM Customer c)"
            R"( JOIN "Order" o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id)"
            R"( JOIN OrderItem oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id)"
            R"( JOIN Product p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id)"
            R"( WHERE c.customer_id = ? ORDER BY o.order_date DESC)",
            customer_id
        );
        return stmt;
    });
    
    // Convert query results to vector of PurchaseHistory
    std::vector<PurchaseHistory> histories;
    for (const auto& row : result.value()) {
        PurchaseHistory ph;
        ph.product_name = row.get<std::string>("p.name");
        ph.order_date = row.get<sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S">>("o.order_date");
        ph.quantity = row.get<int>("oi.quantity");
        histories.push_back(std::move(ph));
    }
    
    return histories;
}

}  // namespace query_customer_purchase_history
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`
2. Creates all required tables using `sqlgen::to_sql` to generate appropriate CREATE TABLE statements
3. Executes a parameterized SQL query joining all four tables to retrieve purchase history
4. Maps the query results to a vector of `PurchaseHistory` objects
5. Returns the complete purchase history for the specified customer in chronological order</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_daily_traffic_metrics {

struct PageVisit {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> visit_id;
  std::string url;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> visit_time;
};

struct DailyTraffic {
  std::string visit_date;
  int total_visits;
  int unique_pages;
};

struct DailyTrafficView {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "DailyTrafficView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string visit_date;
  int total_visits;
  int unique_pages;
};

/**
 * Function: Calculate daily traffic metrics from a log table.
 * 
 * @return List of daily traffic metrics
 */
std::vector<DailyTraffic> query_daily_traffic_metrics() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      | sqlgen::sql(R"(
          SELECT strftime('%Y-%m-%d', visit_time) as visit_date,
                 COUNT(*) as total_visits,
                 COUNT(DISTINCT url) as unique_pages
          FROM PageVisit
          GROUP BY visit_date
          ORDER BY visit_date
      )")
      | sqlgen::to<std::vector<DailyTraffic>>;
}

}  // namespace query_daily_traffic_metrics
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.

2. **SQL Query Execution**: The `sqlgen::sql()` function executes the SQL query that:
   - Extracts the date portion from `visit_time` using SQLite's `strftime` function
   - Groups records by the extracted date
   - Calculates total visits (`COUNT(*)`) and unique pages (`COUNT(DISTINCT url)`)
   - Orders results chronologically by date

3. **Result Conversion**: The `sqlgen::to<std::vector<DailyTraffic>>` operation automatically maps the query results to a vector of `DailyTraffic` objects using reflection, matching column names to struct field names.

4. **Return Value**: The function returns a vector containing daily traffic metrics with date, total visits, and unique pages visited for each day.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_employee_hierarchy {

struct Employee {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> emp_id;
  std::string name;
  std::optional<int> manager_id;
};

struct EmployeeManager {
  std::string employee;
  std::optional<std::string> manager;
};

std::vector<EmployeeManager> query_employee_hierarchy() {
    return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
        .and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<EmployeeManager>>::from("Employee", "e1")
            .left_join("Employee", "e2", "e1.manager_id = e2.emp_id")
            .select("e1.name as employee", "e2.name as manager")
            .execute())
        .value();
}

}  // namespace query_employee_hierarchy
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>

#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace query_high_value_orders {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
};

  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  int customer_id;
  double total_amount;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

struct OrderView {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "HighValueOrderView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string name;
  double total_amount;

      .and_then([](auto conn) {

            .inner_join<Customer>("c", "o.customer_id", "c.customer_id")
            .where("o.total_amount > 1000")
            .order_by("o.total_amount DESC");
        
        return sqlgen::read<std::vector<OrderView>>(query, conn);
      })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace query_high_value_orders

<answer>```cpp
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace query_product_sales_report {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string category;
};

struct Sale {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  double amount;
};

struct CategoryReport {
  std::string category;
  int total_qty;
  double total_amt;
};

struct ProductSalesReport {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "ProductSalesReport";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string category;
  int total_qty;
  double total_amt;
};

std::vector<CategoryReport> query_product_sales_report() {
    try {
        // Connect to the SQLite database
        auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
        
        // Execute the SQL query
        auto result = conn.execute(
            R"(
                SELECT p.category, SUM(s.quantity) as total_qty, SUM(s.amount) as total_amt
                FROM Product p
                INNER JOIN Sale s ON p.product_id = s.product_id
                GROUP BY p.category
                HAVING total_amt > 5000
            )"
        ).value();
        
        // Map the results to CategoryReport objects
        std::vector<CategoryReport> reports;
        for (const auto& row : result) {
            reports.push_back(CategoryReport{
                row.get<std::string>("category"),
                row.get<int>("total_qty"),
                row.get<double>("total_amt")
            });
        }
        
        return reports;
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        // Handle potential errors (e.g., database connection issues)
        // For simplicity, rethrow or handle as needed
        throw std::runtime_error("Database query failed: " + std::string(e.what()));
    }
}

}  // namespace query_product_sales_report
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`
2. Executes the required SQL query with proper JOIN, GROUP BY, and HAVING clauses
3. Maps the query results to `CategoryReport` objects
4. Returns a vector containing the filtered sales report data
5. Includes basic error handling for database operations</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace query_search_books_by_criteria {

struct Book {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> book_id;
  std::string title;
  std::string author;
  int year;
  std::string genre;
  double price;
};

struct BookFilters {
  std::optional<std::string> title_keyword;
  std::optional<int> min_year;
  std::optional<std::string> genre;
  std::optional<double> max_price;
};

std::vector<Book> query_search_books_by_criteria(const BookFilters& filters) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    auto query = sqlgen::read<Book>;
    
    if (filters.title_keyword) {
        query = query | sqlgen::where("title LIKE ?", "%" + *filters.title_keyword + "%");
    }
    
    if (filters.min_year) {
        query = query | sqlgen::where("year >= ?", *filters.min_year);
    }
    
    if (filters.genre) {
        query = query | sqlgen::where("genre = ?", *filters.genre);
    }
    
    if (filters.max_price) {
        query = query | sqlgen::where("price <= ?", *filters.max_price);
    }
    
    return query | conn;
}

}  // namespace query_search_books_by_criteria
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: The function connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **Dynamic Query Construction**: 
   - Starts with a base `sqlgen::read<Book>` query
   - Conditionally adds `WHERE` clauses using `sqlgen::where()` based on non-null filter values
   - Uses parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection

3. **Filter Handling**:
   - `title_keyword`: Uses `LIKE` with wildcards (`%`)
   - `min_year`: Uses `>=` comparison
   - `genre`: Uses exact match (`=`)
   - `max_price`: Uses `<=` comparison

4. **Execution**: The final query is executed against the database connection using the `|` operator.

The solution efficiently handles optional filters while maintaining type safety and preventing SQL injection through parameterized queries.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>

#include <vector>

  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> user_id;

  std::string bio;
  sqlgen::Default<sqlgen::DateTime, sqlgen::current_timestamp> created_at;
  sqlgen::Default<bool, false> is_verified;
};

bool create_complex_table() {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    conn.create_table<UserProfile>();
    return true;

    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace schema_create_complex_table
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace schema_create_trigger_audit_log {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int stock;
};

struct AuditLog {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> log_id;
  std::string table_name;
  int record_id;
  std::string action;
  sqlgen::DateTime timestamp;
};

/**
 * Function: Create a trigger for auditing changes on Product table.
 * 
 * @return true if trigger creation succeeds, false otherwise
 */
bool create_trigger_audit_log() {
  try {
    // Connect to the SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    if (!conn) {
      return false;
    }
    
    // SQL statement to create the audit trigger
    const char* trigger_sql = R"(
      CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS audit_product_update
      AFTER UPDATE ON Product
      BEGIN
          INSERT INTO AuditLog (table_name, record_id, action, timestamp)
          VALUES ('Product', OLD.product_id, 'UPDATE', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
      END;
    )";
    
    // Execute the SQL statement
    return sqlgen::exec(conn, trigger_sql);
  } 
  catch (...) {
    // Handle any exceptions and return false on error
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace schema_create_trigger_audit_log
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: The function uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.

2. **Error Handling**: The connection result is checked, and if it fails, the function returns `false` immediately.

3. **Trigger Creation**: The SQL statement creates an `AFTER UPDATE` trigger named `audit_product_update` on the `Product` table. This trigger:
   - Uses `IF NOT EXISTS` to avoid errors if the trigger already exists
   - Captures the `OLD.product_id` value from the updated record
   - Inserts an audit record with the table name, record ID, action type, and timestamp

4. **Execution**: The trigger SQL is executed using `sqlgen::exec(conn, trigger_sql)`, which returns `true` on success and `false` on failure.

5. **Exception Safety**: All operations are wrapped in a try-catch block to ensure any exceptions are caught and the function returns `false` appropriately.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace schema_manage_indexes {

  struct Product {
    sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
    std::string sku;
    std::string name;
    std::string category;
    double price;
  };

  bool manage_indexes() {
    try {
      auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
      
      // Create Product table
      conn.execute(R"(
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS Product (
          product_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
          sku VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
          name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
          category VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
          price REAL NOT NULL
        )
      )");
      
      // Create indexes with IF NOT EXISTS
      conn.execute("CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_product_category ON Product(category);");
      conn.execute("CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_product_sku ON Product(sku);");
      conn.execute("CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_product_name_price ON Product(name, price);");
      
      return true;
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
      return false;
    }
  }

}  // namespace schema_manage_indexes
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace transaction_transfer_funds {

struct Account {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> account_id;
  double balance;
};

bool transfer_funds(int from_account_id, int to_account_id, double amount) {
    try {
        auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
        conn.begin_transaction();
        
        // Deduct from sender account only if sufficient funds exist
        auto deduct_result = conn.update<Account>()
            .where("account_id = ?", from_account_id)
            .and_where("balance >= ?", amount)
            .set("balance", "balance - ?", amount)
            .execute();
        
        if (deduct_result.rows_affected() != 1) {
            conn.rollback();
            return false;
        }
        
        // Add to receiver account
        auto add_result = conn.update<Account>()
            .where("account_id = ?", to_account_id)
            .set("balance", "balance + ?", amount)
            .execute();
        
        if (add_result.rows_affected() != 1) {
            conn.rollback();
            return false;
        }
        
        conn.commit();
        return true;
    } catch (...) {
        // Handle any exceptions (connection errors, etc.)
        return false;
    }
}

}  // namespace transaction_transfer_funds
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()` to establish a connection to "test_db.sqlite"
2. **Transaction Management**: 
   - `begin_transaction()` starts the transaction
   - `commit()` finalizes successful operations
   - `rollback()` is called if any operation fails
3. **Fund Transfer Logic**:
   - First UPDATE deducts funds only if `balance >= amount` (atomic check)
   - Second UPDATE adds funds to receiver
   - Verifies exactly one row is affected by each UPDATE
4. **Error Handling**:
   - Checks `rows_affected()` after each operation
   - Rolls back transaction on any failure
   - Catches all exceptions and returns `false`

The solution ensures atomicity and consistency through SQLite transactions while using sqlgen's ORM capabilities for type-safe database operations.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
// eas_common.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

struct Student {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<sqlgen::Varchar<10>> SNo;
  sqlgen::Varchar<30> SName;
  int Sex = 1;
  std::optional<sqlgen::DateTime> Birthday;
  std::optional<uint32_t> Major;
  std::optional<double> Scorses = 0.0;
  std::optional<std::string> Remark;
  std::optional<int> Age; // Added for task 2
};

struct Course {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t, sqlgen::auto_incr> CID;
  sqlgen::Varchar<50> CName;
  std::optional<int> Term = 1;
  std::optional<int> CTime;
  int Credit = 0;
};

struct Score {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t, sqlgen::auto_incr> ScID;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<sqlgen::Varchar<10>, Student, "SNo"> SNo;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<uint32_t, Course, "CID"> CID;
  std::optional<double> Score;
  sqlgen::Varchar<1> Grade; // Added for task 1
};

struct Major {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t> MID;
  sqlgen::Varchar<30> MName;
};

const std::string db_path = "data/eas_database.db";

void design_eas_database_without_major() {
  std::filesystem::create_directories("data");
  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then([](auto&& conn) {
        return sqlgen::exec(conn, "PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;");
      })
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Student> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Course> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Score> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .value();
}

void design_eas_database_with_major() {
  design_eas_database_without_major();

  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Major> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .value();
}

// update_eas_table.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

#include "eas_common.hpp"

namespace update_eas_table {

void update_eas_table() {
  design_eas_database_with_major();
  
  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then([](auto&& conn) {
        return sqlgen::exec(conn, "PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;");
      })
      .and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<Score> | sqlgen::if_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<Student> | sqlgen::if_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<Major> | sqlgen::if_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Student> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Score> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .value();
}

} // namespace update_eas_table
```

This solution modifies the `Student` and `Score` structs to include the required columns, then drops and recreates the tables in the correct order to apply the schema changes while removing the `Major` table.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace upsert_user_profile {

  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::optional<std::string> bio;
  std::optional<std::string> avatar_url;
};

bool upsert_user_profile(const UserProfile& u) {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    auto result = conn.execute(
        "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO UserProfile (user_id, bio, avatar_url) VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
        u.user_id, u.bio, u.avatar_url

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int stock;
};

struct Sale {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  double amount;
};

struct SalesSummary {
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string product_name;
  int total_quantity;
  double total_revenue;
};

/**
 * Function: Create a view SalesSummary that aggregates sales by product.
 * 
 * @return true if view creation succeeds, false otherwise
 */
bool create_view_sales_summary() {
    try {
        return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
            .and_then([](auto db) {
                return db.execute(R"(
                    CREATE VIEW IF NOT EXISTS SalesSummary AS
                    SELECT p.name as product_name, SUM(s.quantity) as total_quantity, SUM(s.amount) as total_revenue
                    FROM Sale s
                    JOIN Product p ON s.product_id = p.product_id
                    GROUP BY p.name
                )");
            })
            .has_value();
    } catch (...) {
        return false;
    }
}

}  // namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect` to establish a connection to "test_db.sqlite"
2. **View Creation**: Executes a `CREATE VIEW IF NOT EXISTS` statement that:
   - Joins `Sale` and `Product` tables on `product_id`
   - Groups results by `product_name`
   - Calculates `total_quantity` and `total_revenue` using aggregate functions
3. **Error Handling**: Wraps the operation in a try-catch block to handle any exceptions gracefully
4. **Return Value**: Returns `true` if the view is created successfully, `false` otherwise

The solution leverages SQL's `IF NOT EXISTS` clause to avoid errors if the view already exists, making the function idempotent.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>

namespace right_join_departments_projects {

struct Department {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> department_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Project {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  int department_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct DepProj {
  std::optional<std::string> department_name;
  std::string project_name;
};

std::vector<DepProj> right_join_departments_projects() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<DepProj>> | [](auto query) {
        return query.from<Project>()
                   .right_join<Department>([](const Project& p, const Department& d) {
                     return p.department_id == d.department_id;
                   })
                   .select([](const Project& p, const Department& d) {
                     return DepProj{
                         std::optional<std::string>{d.name},
                         p.name
                     };
                   });
      })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace right_join_departments_projects
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect` to connect to the SQLite database
2. **Right Join**: Performs `RIGHT JOIN` between `Project` (left) and `Department` (right) tables using the join condition `p.department_id == d.department_id`
3. **Result Mapping**: Maps the joined results to `DepProj` struct where:
   - `department_name` is wrapped in `std::optional` (though guaranteed non-null due to RIGHT JOIN)
   - `project_name` comes from the `Project` table
4. **Error Handling**: Uses `.value()` to extract results (assumes successful execution)

The solution correctly implements the specified right join behavior while maintaining the required struct definitions.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace generate_sql_dry_run {

struct A {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string name;
};

struct B {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int a_id;
  double score;
};

std::string generate_sql_dry_run() {
    using namespace sqlgen;
    using namespace sqlgen::literals;

    // Build the query with joins, where conditions, ordering, and limit
    auto query = select_from<A>()
        .join<B>(on: "a.id"_c == "b.a_id"_c)  // Join A and B on their IDs
        .where("b.score"_c > 10 && "a.name"_c.like("%x%"))  // Filter conditions
        .order_by("b.score"_c.desc())  // Order by score descending
        .limit(5);  // Limit to 5 results

    // Generate and return the SQL string for SQLite
    return to_sql<sqlgen::sqlite::Connection>(query);
}

}  // namespace generate_sql_dry_run
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Query Construction**: The function builds a complex SQL query using `sqlgen` DSL:
   - Joins tables `A` and `B` on `A.id = B.a_id`
   - Applies filters: `B.score > 10` AND `A.name contains 'x'`
   - Orders results by `B.score` descending
   - Limits to 5 records

2. **SQL Generation**: Uses `to_sql<sqlite::Connection>` to convert the query object to SQLite-specific SQL syntax without executing it (dry run).

3. **Column References**: Uses `sqlgen::literals` to create type-safe column references (`"column_name"_c`).

The generated SQL will be:
```sql
SELECT * FROM A JOIN B ON a.id = b.a_id WHERE b.score > 10 AND a.name LIKE '%x%' ORDER BY b.score DESC LIMIT 5
```</answer>

std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> analyze_temporal_data(const std::vector<Event>& events) {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create temporary table
    auto create_stmt = sqlgen::create_table<Event>();
    std::string create_sql = create_stmt.to_sql();
    size_t pos = create_sql.find("CREATE TABLE");
    if (pos != std::string::npos) {
      create_sql.replace(pos, 12, "CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE events_temp");
    }
    conn.and_then([&](auto& db) {
      return db.execute(create_sql);
    });
    
    // Insert events
    conn.and_then([&](auto& db) {
      for (const auto& event : events) {
        auto insert_stmt = sqlgen::insert_into<Event>().values(event);
        db.execute(insert_stmt.to_sql());
      }
      return db;
    });
    
    // Execute SELECT query
    std::string select_sql = R"(
      SELECT 
        event_id,
        event_name,
        DAY(start_time) as start_day,
        MONTH(start_time) as start_month,
        YEAR(start_time) as start_year,
        HOUR(start_time) as start_hour,
        MINUTE(start_time) as start_minute,
        SECOND(start_time) as start_second,
        WEEKDAY(start_time) as start_weekday,
        UNIXEPOCH(start_time) as start_epoch,
        DAYS_BETWEEN(start_time, end_time) as duration_days
      FROM events_temp
      WHERE end_time IS NOT NULL
    )";
    
    auto result = conn.and_then([&](auto& db) {
      return db.execute(select_sql);
    }).and_then([](auto&& cursor) -> std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> {
      std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> analyses;
      while (cursor.next()) {
        TemporalAnalysis analysis;
        analysis.event_id = cursor.get<int>("event_id");
        analysis.event_name = cursor.get<std::string>("event_name");
        analysis.start_day = cursor.get<int>("start_day");
        analysis.start_month = cursor.get<int>("start_month");
        analysis.start_year = cursor.get<int>("start_year");
        analysis.start_hour = cursor.get<int>("start_hour");
        analysis.start_minute = cursor.get<int>("start_minute");
        analysis.start_second = cursor.get<int>("start_second");
        analysis.start_weekday = cursor.get<int>("start_weekday");
        analysis.start_epoch = cursor.get<int64_t>("start_epoch");
        if (cursor.has_column("duration_days")) {
          analysis.duration_days = cursor.get<double>("duration_days");
        }
        analyses.push_back(std::move(analysis));
      }
      return analyses;
    });
    
    return result.value();
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    throw std::runtime_error("Failed to analyze temporal data: " + std::string(e.what()));
  }
}

This code should handle the requirements. However, there are a few potential issues:

1. The temporary table name "events_temp" must be unique and not conflict with existing tables.
2. The code assumes that the SQLite database "test_db.sqlite" exists. If it doesn't, the connection will fail.
3. Error handling is minimal; in a production environment, you'd want more robust error handling.
4. The code uses string manipulation to create the temporary table, which is error-prone if the CREATE TABLE SQL format changes.

But given the problem constraints and the use of sqlgen, this should be a valid solution.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <stdexcept>

namespace analyze_temporal_data {

struct Event {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> event_id;
  std::string event_name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> start_time;
  std::optional<sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S">> end_time;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

struct TemporalAnalysis {
  int event_id;
  std::string event_name;
  int start_day;
  int start_month;
  int start_year;
  int start_hour;
  int start_minute;
  int start_second;
  int start_weekday;
  int64_t start_epoch;
  std::optional<double> duration_days;
};

std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> analyze_temporal_data(const std::vector<Event>& events) {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create temporary table with the same structure as Event
    auto create_stmt = sqlgen::create_table<Event>();
    std::string create_sql = create_stmt.to_sql();
    
    // Convert to temporary table and rename
    size_t pos = create_sql.find("CREATE TABLE");
    if (pos == std::string::npos) {
      throw std::runtime_error("Invalid CREATE TABLE statement");
    }
    create_sql.replace(pos, 12, "CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE events_temp");
    
    // Execute table creation
    auto create_result = conn.and_then([&](auto& db) {
      return db.execute(create_sql);
    });
    
    if (!create_result) {
      throw std::runtime_error("Failed to create temporary table");
    }
    
    // Insert events into the temporary table
    auto insert_result = conn.and_then([&](auto& db) {
      for (const auto& event : events) {
        auto insert_stmt = sqlgen::insert_into<Event>().values(event);
        auto insert_res = db.execute(insert_stmt.to_sql());
        if (!insert_res) {
          throw std::runtime_error("Failed to insert event");
        }
      }
      return db;
    });
    
    if (!insert_result) {
      throw std::runtime_error("Failed to insert events");
    }
    
    // Execute the temporal analysis query
    std::string select_sql = R"(
      SELECT 
        event_id,
        event_name,
        CAST(strftime('%d', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_day,
        CAST(strftime('%m', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_month,
        CAST(strftime('%Y', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_year,
        CAST(strftime('%H', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_hour,
        CAST(strftime('%M', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_minute,
        CAST(strftime('%S', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_second,
        CAST(strftime('%w', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_weekday,
        CAST(strftime('%s', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_epoch,
        CASE 
          WHEN end_time IS NOT NULL THEN 
            (julianday(end_time) - julianday(start_time)) 
          ELSE NULL 
        END as duration_days
      FROM events_temp
      WHERE end_time IS NOT NULL
    )";
    
    auto query_result = conn.and_then([&](auto& db) {
      return db.execute(select_sql);
    }).and_then([](auto&& cursor) -> std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> {
      std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> analyses;
      
      while (cursor.next()) {
        TemporalAnalysis analysis;
        analysis.event_id = cursor.get<int>("event_id");
        analysis.event_name = cursor.get<std::string>("event_name");
        analysis.start_day = cursor.get<int>("start_day");
        analysis.start_month = cursor.get<int>("start_month");
        analysis.start_year = cursor.get<int>("start_year");
        analysis.start_hour = cursor.get<int>("start_hour");
        analysis.start_minute = cursor.get<int>("start_minute");
        analysis.start_second = cursor.get<int>("start_second");
        analysis.start_weekday = cursor.get<int>("start_weekday");
        analysis.start_epoch = cursor.get<int64_t>("start_epoch");
        
        if (cursor.has_column("duration_days") && !cursor.is_null("duration_days")) {
          analysis.duration_days = cursor.get<double>("duration_days");
        }
        
        analyses.push_back(std::move(analysis));
      }
      
      return analyses;
    });
    
    if (!query_result) {
      throw std::runtime_error("Failed to execute query");
    }
    
    return query_result.value();
    
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    throw std::runtime_error("Temporal analysis failed: " + std::string(e.what()));
  }
}

}  // namespace analyze_temporal_data
```

**Key Features of the Solution:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()` to connect to the SQLite database file.

2. **Temporary Table Creation**: Dynamically converts the standard `CREATE TABLE` statement to a `CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE` statement.

3. **Data Insertion**: Iterates through the input events and inserts them into the temporary table using `sqlgen::insert_into()`.

4. **Date Operations**: Uses SQLite's date/time functions (`strftime`, `julianday`) to extract components and calculate durations.

5. **Result Mapping**: Converts query results to the `TemporalAnalysis` struct, handling optional duration days.

6. **Error Handling**: Includes comprehensive exception handling for database operations.

7. **SQLite-Specific Functions**: Uses SQLite's date functions which are more reliable than custom SQL functions across different SQLite versions.

The solution demonstrates proper use of the sqlgen library while handling the complex date/time operations required by the problem.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <stdexcept>

namespace upsert_inventory_items {

struct Inventory {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  std::string name;
  int quantity;
};

bool upsert_inventory_items(const std::vector<Inventory>& items) {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Perform upsert operation using OR REPLACE
    conn.write(items).or_replace().value();
    
    // Read back all items from the Inventory table
    auto read_items = conn.read<std::vector<Inventory>>("SELECT * FROM Inventory").value();
    
    // Verify the number of items matches
    if (items.size() != read_items.size()) {
      return false;
    }
    
    // Create maps for efficient comparison by primary key
    std::unordered_map<int, Inventory> original_map, read_map;
    for (const auto& item : items) {
      original_map[item.item_id.value()] = item;
    }
    for (const auto& item : read_items) {
      read_map[item.item_id.value()] = item;
    }
    
    // Compare each item in the original map with the read map
    for (const auto& [id, original_item] : original_map) {
      if (read_map.find(id) == read_map.end()) {
        return false; // Item not found in database
      }
      
      const auto& read_item = read_map[id];
      if (original_item.name != read_item.name || 
          original_item.quantity != read_item.quantity) {
        return false; // Item data mismatch
      }
    }
    
    return true; // All verifications passed
    
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    return false; // Any exception indicates failure
  }
}

}  // namespace upsert_inventory_items
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **Upsert Operation**: Uses `conn.write(items).or_replace().value()` to perform an INSERT OR REPLACE operation for each item in the input vector. The `.or_replace()` modifier ensures existing records with matching primary keys are updated.

3. **Verification**: 
   - Reads all items back from the database using a SELECT query
   - Compares the count of original and read items
   - Creates hash maps for efficient comparison by primary key
   - Verifies each item's data integrity (name and quantity)

4. **Error Handling**: Wraps the entire operation in a try-catch block, returning `false` on any exception.

The solution ensures data consistency by verifying that the upsert operation correctly inserts new items and replaces existing ones while maintaining data integrity.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace limit_recent_events {

struct Event {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> event_id;
  std::string name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

std::vector<Event> limit_recent_events(size_t n = 10) {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<Event>> | 
                sqlgen::order_by("created_at", sqlgen::desc) |
                sqlgen::limit(n))
      .value();
}
}  // namespace limit_recent_events
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace mathematical_operations_analysis {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int quantity;
  double discount_rate;
};

struct MathAnalysis {
  int product_id;
  std::string name;
  double abs_price;
  double ceil_price;
  double floor_price;
  double rounded_price;
  double exp_discount;
  double ln_price;
  double log2_quantity;
  double sin_discount;
  double cos_discount;
  double tan_discount;
  double sqrt_quantity;
};

std::vector<MathAnalysis> analyze_mathematical_operations(const std::vector<Product>& products) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect();
  sqlgen::write(conn, products);
  
  const std::string query = R"(
    SELECT 
      product_id,
      name,
      ABS(price) as abs_price,
      CEIL(price) as ceil_price,
      FLOOR(price) as floor_price,
      ROUND(price, 2) as rounded_price,
      EXP(discount_rate / 100) as exp_discount,
      LN(price) as ln_price,
      LOG2(quantity) as log2_quantity,
      SIN(discount_rate) as sin_discount,
      COS(discount_rate) as cos_discount,
      TAN(discount_rate) as tan_discount,
      SQRT(quantity) as sqrt_quantity
    FROM Product
  )";
  
  return sqlgen::read<std::vector<MathAnalysis>>(conn, query);
}

}  // namespace mathematical_operations_analysis
```

This solution:
1. Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()` to establish a database connection
2. Writes the input products to the database using `sqlgen::write()`
3. Executes a SQL query with all required mathematical operations
4. Maps the results to the `MathAnalysis` struct using `sqlgen::read()`
5. Returns the comprehensive analysis of mathematical operations</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace trim_and_length_validation {

struct UserProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string name;
  std::string email;
};

struct ValidatedProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string trimmed_name;
  std::string trimmed_email;
  int name_length;
  int email_length;
};

std::vector<ValidatedProfile> trim_and_length_validation() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then([](auto conn) {
        const auto query = R"(
          SELECT user_id, 
                 TRIM(name) as trimmed_name,
                 TRIM(email) as trimmed_email,
                 LENGTH(TRIM(name)) as name_length,
                 LENGTH(TRIM(email)) as email_length
          FROM UserProfile
        )";
        return sqlgen::read<std::vector<ValidatedProfile>>(conn, query);
      })
      .value();
}
}  // namespace trim_and_length_validation
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`
2. Executes a SQL query that:
   - Trims whitespace from `name` and `email` fields using `TRIM()`
   - Computes the length of trimmed values using `LENGTH()`
   - Selects the original `user_id` alongside processed values
3. Uses `sqlgen::read` to map query results to `ValidatedProfile` structs
4. Returns a vector containing all validated user profiles

The solution handles the core requirements of trimming, length calculation, and proper data mapping while maintaining clean, readable code structure.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <stdexcept>

namespace calculate_order_metrics {

struct OrderItem {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  int order_id;
  int product_id;
  double unit_price;
  int quantity;
  std::optional<double> discount_rate;
  std::optional<double> tax_rate;
};

struct OrderMetrics {
  int item_id;
  int order_id;
  int product_id;
  double unit_price;
  int quantity;
  double discount_rate;
  double tax_rate;
  double gross_total;
  double discounted_total;
  double final_total;
  double unit_cost;
  int quantity_group;
  double estimated_profit;
};

std::vector<OrderMetrics> calculate_order_metrics_impl(const std::vector<OrderItem>& items) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  try {
    // Connect to an in-memory SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlite::connect(":memory:")
      .and_then(create_table<OrderItem>)
      .and_then(write(items.begin(), items.end()));

    if (!conn) {
      throw std::runtime_error("Failed to connect to database or create table");
    }

    // Execute the query with arithmetic operations
    auto result = conn.value().and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<OrderMetrics>> << R"(
      SELECT 
        item_id,
        order_id,
        product_id,
        unit_price,
        quantity,
        COALESCE(discount_rate, 0) as discount_rate,
        COALESCE(tax_rate, 0) as tax_rate,
        (unit_price * quantity) as gross_total,
        (unit_price * quantity * (1 - COALESCE(discount_rate, 0)/100)) as discounted_total,
        (unit_price * quantity * (1 - COALESCE(discount_rate, 0)/100) * (1 + COALESCE(tax_rate, 0)/100)) as final_total,
        (unit_price * quantity / quantity) as unit_cost,
        (quantity % 10) as quantity_group,
        (unit_price * 0.3) as estimated_profit
      FROM OrderItem
    )");

    if (!result) {
      throw std::runtime_error("Query execution failed");
    }

    return result.value();
  }
  catch (const std::exception& e) {
    throw std::runtime_error(std::string("Error in calculate_order_metrics_impl: ") + e.what());
  }
}

}  // namespace calculate_order_metrics
```

This solution:
1. Creates an in-memory SQLite database using sqlgen
2. Dynamically creates the OrderItem table based on the struct definition
3. Inserts all provided OrderItem records
4. Executes a SQL query with arithmetic operations to calculate business metrics
5. Handles optional fields using COALESCE for null safety
6. Maps the query results to the OrderMetrics struct
7. Includes proper error handling for database operations
8. Uses sqlgen's fluent API for clean, readable database operations

The solution demonstrates practical SQL arithmetic operations while maintaining type safety and proper error handling.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace read_single_record_by_id {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
  std::string email;
};

/**
 * description: read_single_record_by_id.
 * This function demonstrates how to read a single record by id using sqlgen read API.
 * It returns the matched row from the Customer table.
 * Returns: Customer.
 */
Customer read_single_record_by_id(int id) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  return conn.read<Customer>(id);
}

}  // namespace read_single_record_by_id
```</answer>

<think>Okay, I need to write a function called join_orders_customers_payments using the sqlgen library. Let me start by understanding what the problem requires.

First, the function should connect to an SQLite database named test_db.sqlite. Then, it needs to perform inner joins between the Orders, Customers, and Payments tables. The goal is to return a consolidated dataset with specific columns: order_id, customer_name, and amount, ordered by amount descending.

Looking at the table structures:

- Customer has customer_id (primary key) and name.
- Order has order_id (primary key), customer_id (foreign key to Customer), and order_date.
- Payment has payment_id (primary key), order_id (foreign key to Order), and amount.

So, the joins need to connect these tables. The consolidated struct has order_id, customer_name, and amount. 

The steps are:

1. Connect to the database.
2. Perform an inner join between Orders and Customers on customer_id.
3. Then join Payments with the result on order_id.

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace join_orders_customers_payments {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  int customer_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

struct Payment {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> payment_id;
  int order_id;
  double amount;
};

struct Consolidated {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string customer_name;
  double amount;
};

std::vector<Consolidated> join_orders_customers_payments() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then([](auto conn) {
        return conn.read<std::vector<Consolidated>>(
            sqlgen::select_from<Order>(conn)
                .join<Customer>(sqlgen::JoinType::inner, 
                    [](const Order& o, const Customer& c) {
                      return o.customer_id == c.customer_id;
                    })
                .join<Payment>(sqlgen::JoinType::inner,
                    [](const auto& joined, const Payment& p) {
                      return joined.order.order_id == p.order_id;
                    })
                .order_by("amount", sqlgen::Order::desc)
                .transform([](const auto& row) {
                  return Consolidated{
                    .order_id = row.order.order_id,
                    .customer_name = row.customer.name,
                    .amount = row.payment.amount
                  };
                })
        );
      })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace join_orders_customers_payments
```

2. Perform inner joins between Orders, Customers, and Payments tables
3. Select and map the required columns to the `Consolidated` struct
4. Order the results by payment amount in descending order
5. Return the consolidated data as a vector of `Consolidated` objects</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace replace_text_in_notes {

struct Note {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string content;
};

struct UpdatedNote {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string updated_content;
};

std::vector<UpdatedNote> replace_text_in_notes() {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Execute the update query to replace "foo" with "bar" in the content column
  sqlgen::update<Note>().set("content", "REPLACE(content, 'foo', 'bar')").execute(conn);
  
  // Retrieve the updated notes from the database
  auto notes = sqlgen::read<std::vector<Note>>().from(conn).value();
  
  // Convert the retrieved notes to the UpdatedNote format
  std::vector<UpdatedNote> updated_notes;
  for (const auto& note : notes) {
    updated_notes.push_back(UpdatedNote{
      note.note_id,
      note.content  // Content now contains the replaced text
    });
  }
  
  return updated_notes;
}

}  // namespace replace_text_in_notes
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`
2. Uses SQL's `REPLACE()` function to substitute "foo" with "bar" in all notes
3. Retrieves the updated records using `sqlgen::read`
4. Converts the results to the required `UpdatedNote` format
5. Returns the vector containing the updated notes for verification</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace cascade_delete_demo {

struct Parent {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> parent_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Child {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> child_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Parent, "parent_id"> parent_id;
  std::string note;
};

bool cascade_delete_demo() {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    conn.execute("PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;"); // Enable foreign key constraints
    
    // Create tables
    conn.create_table<Parent>();
    conn.create_table<Child>();
    
    // Insert test data
    conn.insert(Parent{1, "Parent1"});
    conn.insert(Child{1, 1, "Test Note"});
    
    // Delete parent record
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM Parent WHERE parent_id = ?", 1);
    
    // Verify child was cascaded deleted
    auto count_result = conn.value("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Child WHERE parent_id = ?", 1);
    int remaining_children = count_result.as<int>();
    
    return remaining_children == 0;
  }
  catch (...) {
    return false; // Return false on any error
  }
}

}  // namespace cascade_delete_demo
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Database Connection:** Connects to SQLite database "test_db.sqlite" and enables foreign key constraints
2. **Table Creation:** Creates `Parent` and `Child` tables with proper foreign key constraints
3. **Data Insertion:** Inserts sample parent-child records with matching IDs
4. **Cascade Delete:** Deletes the parent record and verifies child records are automatically removed
5. **Verification:** Returns `true` if child records are successfully deleted, `false` otherwise</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace column_is_null_checks {

struct Profile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> profile_id;
  std::string display_name;
  std::optional<std::string> bio;
  std::optional<std::string> avatar_url;
};

struct Result {
  int profile_id;
  std::string display_name;
};

std::vector<Result> column_is_null_checks() {
    using namespace sqlgen;
    using namespace sqlgen::literals;

    return sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
        .and_then(read<std::vector<Profile>> | where("bio"_c.is_null() || "avatar_url"_c.is_null()))
        .transform([](const auto& profiles) {
            std::vector<Result> results;
            for (const auto& profile : profiles) {
                results.push_back(Result{
                    profile.profile_id,
                    profile.display_name
                });
            }
            return results;
        })
        .value();
}

}  // namespace column_is_null_checks
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.

2. **Query Construction**: 
   - `read<std::vector<Profile>>` reads all Profile records
   - `where("bio"_c.is_null() || "avatar_url"_c.is_null())` filters for records where either bio or avatar_url is NULL
   - The `_c` suffix creates column references using user-defined literals

3. **Result Transformation**:
   - Converts the vector of Profile objects to a vector of Result objects
   - Only extracts `profile_id` and `display_name` from each Profile

4. **Error Handling**: Uses `.value()` to extract the result, assuming successful execution (as required by the problem constraints)

The solution efficiently filters records with missing optional fields and returns the requested data in the specified format.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

namespace update_set_operations {

struct Account {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> account_id;
  double balance;
  std::string status;
};

bool update_set_operations() {
  try {
    // Connect to SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create Account table
    sqlgen::write::create_table<Account>(conn);
    
    // Insert initial data
    sqlgen::write::insert(conn, Account{1, 100.0, "active"});
    sqlgen::write::insert(conn, Account{2, 200.0, "inactive"});
    
    // Update account 1: set balance and status
    auto update1 = sqlgen::write::update<Account>(conn)
        .where("account_id = ?", 1)
        .set("balance", 150.0)
        .set("status", "pending")
        .execute();
    
    if (update1.affected_rows() != 1) return false;
    
    // Update account 2: set balance using expression
    auto update2 = sqlgen::write::update<Account>(conn)
        .where("account_id = ?", 2)
        .set("balance", "balance + 50.0")
        .execute();
    
    if (update2.affected_rows() != 1) return false;
    
    // Verify changes
    auto accounts = sqlgen::read::all<Account>(conn);
    
    if (accounts.size() != 2) return false;
    
    // Find and verify account 1
    auto acc1_it = std::find_if(accounts.begin(), accounts.end(),
        [](const Account& acc) { return acc.account_id == 1; });
    
    if (acc1_it == accounts.end()) return false;
    if (acc1_it->balance != 150.0 || acc1_it->status != "pending") return false;
    
    // Find and verify account 2
    auto acc2_it = std::find_if(accounts.begin(), accounts.end(),
        [](const Account& acc) { return acc.account_id == 2; });
    
    if (acc2_it == accounts.end()) return false;
    if (acc2_it->balance != 250.0 || acc2_it->status != "inactive") return false;
    
    return true;
    
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace update_set_operations
```

This solution:
1. Connects to SQLite database "test_db.sqlite"
2. Creates the Account table with proper schema
3. Inserts initial test data
4. Performs two update operations using different SET patterns
5. Verifies the updates persisted correctly
6. Handles exceptions gracefully
7. Returns boolean indicating operation success</answer>

JoinAnalysis analyze_department_employees_impl(const std::vector<Department>& depts, const std::vector<Employee>& emps) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to in-memory SQLite database

  // Create tables
  conn.and_then(create_table<Department>)
      .and_then(create_table<Employee>)
      .value();

  // Insert data
  conn.and_then(insert(depts))
      .and_then(insert(emps))
      .value();

  // Perform INNER JOIN
  auto inner_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Department d
    INNER JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
  )";
  auto inner_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(inner_query).value();

  // Perform LEFT JOIN
  auto left_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Employee e
    LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
  )";
  auto left_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(left_query).value();

  // Perform RIGHT JOIN (simulated with LEFT JOIN reversed)
  auto right_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Department d
    LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
  )";
  auto right_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(right_query).value();

  // Perform FULL JOIN (UNION of LEFT and RIGHT joins)
  auto full_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Employee e
    LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
    UNION
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Department d
    LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
  )";
  auto full_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(full_query).value();

  return JoinAnalysis{
      std::move(inner_results),
      std::move(left_results),
      std::move(right_results),
      std::move(full_results)
  };
}

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>

namespace analyze_department_employees {

struct Department {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> department_id;
  std::string department_name;
  std::string location;
};

struct Employee {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> employee_id;
  int department_id;
  std::string first_name;
  std::string last_name;
  double salary;
};

struct JoinResult {
  std::optional<std::string> department_name;
  std::optional<std::string> first_name;
  std::optional<std::string> last_name;
  std::optional<double> salary;
};

struct JoinAnalysis {
  std::vector<JoinResult> inner_join_results;
  std::vector<JoinResult> left_join_results;
  std::vector<JoinResult> right_join_results;
  std::vector<JoinResult> full_join_results;
};

JoinAnalysis analyze_department_employees_impl(const std::vector<Department>& depts, const std::vector<Employee>& emps) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to an in-memory SQLite database
  auto conn = sqlite::connect(":memory:").value();

  // Create tables
  conn.and_then(create_table<Department>)
      .and_then(create_table<Employee>)
      .value();

  // Insert data
  conn.and_then(insert(depts))
      .and_then(insert(emps))
      .value();

  // Perform INNER JOIN
  auto inner_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Department d
    INNER JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
  )";
  auto inner_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(inner_query).value();

  // Perform LEFT JOIN
  auto left_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Employee e
    LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
  )";
  auto left_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(left_query).value();

  // Perform RIGHT JOIN (simulated using LEFT JOIN with reversed tables)
  auto right_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Department d
    LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
  )";
  auto right_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(right_query).value();

  // Perform FULL JOIN using UNION of LEFT and RIGHT joins
  auto full_query = R"(
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Employee e
    LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
    UNION
    SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
    FROM Department d
    LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
  )";
  auto full_results = conn.read<std::vector<JoinResult>>(full_query).value();

  return JoinAnalysis{
      std::move(inner_results),
      std::move(left_results),
      std::move(right_results),
      std::move(full_results)
  };
}

}  // namespace analyze_department_employees

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace concat_function {

struct Person {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string first_name;
  std::string last_name;
};

struct FullName {
  std::string full_name;
};

inline auto concat_function() {
    return sqlgen::select_from<Person>(
        sqlgen::concat(Person::first_name, sqlgen::string(" "), Person::last_name).as("full_name")
    );
}

}  // namespace concat_function
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Struct Definitions:**

   - `FullName` struct is used to map the query result with a `full_name` field.

   - Applies `sqlgen::concat` to concatenate `first_name`, a space (`sqlgen::string(" ")`), and `last_name`.
   - Uses `.as("full_name")` to alias the concatenated result, matching the `FullName` struct's field.

3. **Return Value:**
   - Returns the constructed query object, which can be executed to retrieve results mapped to `FullName` structs.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace delete_with_complex_condition {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int age;
  std::string status;
};

/**
 * This function demonstrates deleting with complex conditions.
 * Returns: std::string.
 */
inline std::string delete_with_complex_condition() {
    return sqlgen::delete_from<User>()
        .where(User::age < 18 || User::status == "inactive")
        .to_sql();
}

}  // namespace delete_with_complex_condition
```

**Explanation:**
1. **User Struct Definition:** The `User` struct is defined with `id` as a primary key, `age` as an integer, and `status` as a string.
2. **Complex Condition Construction:** The `where()` method combines two conditions using the logical OR operator (`||`):
   - `User::age < 18` (age less than 18)
   - `User::status == "inactive"` (status equals "inactive")
3. **SQL Generation:** The `to_sql()` method converts the constructed query to a SQL string, which will look like:
   ```sql
   DELETE FROM users WHERE age < 18 OR status = 'inactive';
   ```
4. **Return Value:** The function returns the generated SQL string.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace get_top_products {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string product_name;
  std::string category;
  double price;
};

struct Sales {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  sqlgen::Date sale_date;
};

struct TopProduct {
  int product_id;
  std::string product_name;
  std::string category;
  double total_sales;
};

std::vector<TopProduct> get_top_products(size_t limit_count = 10) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    auto query = sqlgen::from<Product>()
        .inner_join<Sales>([](const auto& p, const auto& s) {
            return p.product_id == s.product_id;
        })
        .select(
            sqlgen::col("product_id"),
            sqlgen::col("product_name"),
            sqlgen::col("category"),
            sqlgen::sum(sqlgen::col("price") * sqlgen::col("quantity")).as("total_sales")
        )
        .group_by("product_id", "product_name", "category")
        .order_by("total_sales", sqlgen::desc)
        .limit(limit_count);
    
    std::vector<TopProduct> result;
    for (const auto& row : conn.execute(query)) {
        TopProduct tp;
        tp.product_id = row.get<int>("product_id");
        tp.product_name = row.get<std::string>("product_name");
        tp.category = row.get<std::string>("category");
        tp.total_sales = row.get<double>("total_sales");
        result.push_back(tp);
    }
    
    return result;
}

}  // namespace get_top_products
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`
2. Joins Product and Sales tables on `product_id`
3. Calculates total sales using `SUM(price * quantity)`
4. Groups results by product attributes
5. Orders by total sales in descending order
6. Applies the limit to get top N products
7. Maps results to `TopProduct` structs for return</answer>

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
